input.prompt:
Audit mode. Review the current branch diff (`git diff main...HEAD`) on branch chore/claude-housekeeping — it renumbers CONTRIBUTING.md working agreements (G section 32-34 → 33-35, H section 35-38 → 36-39; F keeps 30-32) to fix a duplicate rule #32 (issue #547), and updates cross-references in CLAUDE.md ("rule 38" → 39, "37 rules" → 39) and docs/contributing.md (37 → 39). It also updates .claude/agents/*.md and .claude/skills/ files.

Check specifically:
1. CONTRIBUTING.md numbered rules are now strictly increasing with no duplicates or gaps (1-39, plus the 4a sub-rule).
2. No remaining in-repo reference to a working-agreement number that shifted (grep docs/, CLAUDE.md, .claude/, .github/ for citations of rules 32-39 by number and verify each points at the right rule content — e.g. progress.md's "rule 32" should still mean the F-section read-modify-write rule, which did NOT shift).
3. All relative links added in the diff resolve (e.g. .claude/agents/docs-consistency-guard.md links to docs/progress.md and context/post-v1-roadmap.md; CONTRIBUTING rule 36 references .claude/skills/security-scan/).
4. The rule-count claims ("39 rules") match the actual count of numbered rules in CONTRIBUTING.md.

Report findings with the standard 🔴/🟡/✅ verdict format.

content.text:
`high effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify (recall-biased) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, recall-biased)

Dedup near-duplicates (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). For
each remaining candidate, run **one verifier** via the Agent tool:
give it the diff, the relevant file(s), and the candidate; it returns exactly
one of **CONFIRMED / PLAUSIBLE / REFUTED**.

**PLAUSIBLE by default** — do not refute a candidate for being "speculative" or
"depends on runtime state" when the state is realistic: concurrency races,
nil/undefined on a rare-but-reachable path (error handler, cold cache, missing
optional field), falsy-zero treated as missing, off-by-one on a boundary the
code does not exclude, retry storms / partial failures, regex/allowlist that
lost an anchor. These are PLAUSIBLE.

**REFUTED** only when constructible from the code: factually wrong (quote the
actual line); provably impossible (type/constant/invariant — show it); already
handled in this diff (cite the guard); or pure style with no observable effect.

Keep **CONFIRMED and PLAUSIBLE**. Drop REFUTED.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.

input.prompt:
You are a code-review finder agent. Scope: `git diff main...HEAD` in /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ (branch chore/claude-housekeeping; 8 changed files — .claude/agents/*.md, .claude/skills/**/SKILL.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/contributing.md).

Angle A+B: line-by-line scan + removed-behavior audit. Read the full diff hunk by hunk, and the enclosing sections of each changed file. These are agent/skill instruction files and project docs, so "bugs" are:
- **Broken embedded commands**: every bash/gh/az/python command added in the diff must be syntactically valid and reference real things. Verify (read-only execution is allowed for verification, e.g. --help, gh api with harmless GETs is OK but prefer static checks): `gh api repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/rules/branches/main` shape, `gh issue create --milestone` flag existence, `pre-commit run betterleaks --all-files` (does .pre-commit-config.yaml define a hook with id `betterleaks`?), `pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt`, `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` scripts, `python -m backend.scripts.e2e_smoke` / `seed_dev` invocation context (repo root vs backend/), `docker compose` vs `docker-compose` (which does this repo's docs/tooling use?), `az keyvault list --resource-group dataq-rg` (RG name per deploy/terraform), mypy/black/ruff/bandit/pytest command paths in .claude/agents/qa-verifier.md vs .github/workflows/ci.yml.
- **Factually wrong claims**: statements in the changed lines that contradict the repo (wrong file paths, wrong rule numbers, wrong issue numbers, wrong defaults).
- **Removed behavior**: for guidance the diff DELETED (e.g. the old docs/TODO.md maintenance section in .claude/agents/docs-consistency-guard.md, the old plain-sentence title rule in .claude/skills/gh-issue-from-finding/SKILL.md), check nothing still references the removed mechanism elsewhere in the repo (grep for TODO.md references, plain-title assumptions).

Return up to 6 candidate findings, each as: file, line (in the new file), one-line summary, concrete failure_scenario (what breaks when an agent/human follows the instruction). Pass through every candidate with a nameable failure scenario — do not self-censor borderline ones.

You are a code-review finder agent. Scope: `git diff main...HEAD` in /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ (branch chore/claude-housekeeping; changed files: .claude/agents/{docs-consistency-guard,migration-safety,qa-verifier}.md, .claude/skills/{gh-issue-from-finding,security-scan}/SKILL.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/contributing.md).

Angle C (cross-file tracer) + Conventions:
1. **Renumber integrity**: CONTRIBUTING.md was renumbered (G: 32-34 → 33-35, H: 35-38 → 36-39). Grep the ENTIRE repo (docs/, .github/, .claude/, backend/, frontend/, context/, *.md, *.py, *.yml) for any citation of working-agreement/rule numbers 24-39 (patterns like "rule 32", "rule #38", "agreement #35", "working-agreement") and verify each citation now points at the correct rule content in the new numbering. Especially: docs/progress.md "rule 32" (should be F read-modify-write, unchanged), CLAUDE.md rule-39 references, .claude/agents/migration-safety.md #30, any ADR citing a rule number.
2. **Reference existence**: every path, file, issue number, and anchor referenced by the CHANGED lines must exist: .claude/skills/security-scan/ (referenced from CONTRIBUTING rule 36), backend/tests/support/adversarial.py, backend/scripts/e2e_smoke.py, scripts/check-typecheck-deps.py, frontend package.json scripts (format:check, lint, typecheck, test), docs/progress.md, context/post-v1-roadmap.md, relative links inside .claude/agents/*.md (they use ../../ prefixes — do they resolve from the agent file's location?), issue #547/#8/#9 states, GH labels/milestones named in gh-issue-from-finding SKILL.md.
3. **Rule-count claim**: CLAUDE.md and docs/contributing.md now say "39 rules" — count the actual top-level numbered rules in CONTRIBUTING.md and check.
4. **Conventions (CLAUDE.md)**: read the repo-root CLAUDE.md; check the diff for clear violations of its rules (e.g. §11 what-not-to-do list, credential rules, "don't hardcode Azure resource names in business logic" — does naming dataq-rg in a skill violate anything? Only flag with the exact quoted rule).
5. **Internal contradictions between changed files**: e.g. does qa-verifier.md's instruction conflict with CONTRIBUTING or CLAUDE.md? Does security-scan SKILL.md contradict rule 36/37/38/39 text? Does gh-issue-from-finding's new title rule contradict any other doc still saying plain titles?

Return up to 6 candidate findings: file, line, one-line summary, concrete failure_scenario. Surface borderline candidates rather than dropping them.

You are a code-review finder agent. Scope: `git diff main...HEAD` in /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ (branch chore/claude-housekeeping; changed files are markdown: .claude/agents/{docs-consistency-guard,migration-safety,qa-verifier}.md, .claude/skills/{gh-issue-from-finding,security-scan}/SKILL.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/contributing.md).

Angles: Reuse / Simplification / Efficiency / Altitude, applied to instruction files:
1. **Duplication**: does new content in the diff duplicate guidance that already lives elsewhere (existing plugin agents like pr-test-analyzer, the /verify or /security-review built-in skills, CONTRIBUTING rule 4a text, docs/deployment or runbook docs)? Duplicated instructions drift apart — name the existing home and what overlaps. Notably: qa-verifier.md re-lists CI gate commands that .github/workflows/ci.yml defines; security-scan SKILL.md re-lists audit commands CI runs — is the duplication justified (local mirror) or should it reference a single source?
2. **Simplification**: redundant or contradictory instructions within a single changed file; overly specific detail that will rot (hardcoded values like resource-group names, URLs, credential expiry dates, image tags inside agent/skill files — each is a staleness liability; flag ones likely to rot fastest and suggest pointing at the durable source instead).
3. **Efficiency**: instructions that make an agent do wasted work (e.g. qa-verifier told to always run the full battery when diff-scoped runs would do; security-scan steps that could use one API call instead of several) — only flag if the waste is material.
4. **Altitude**: is each fix at the right depth? E.g. CLAUDE.md now says "rule 39 (numbered 38 pre-#547)" — is a breadcrumb the right depth or a bandaid? docs-consistency-guard's new deferred-work-homes section — right generalization? gh-issue-from-finding hardcodes milestone titles — should it derive them?

Return up to 6 candidate findings: file, line, one-line summary, failure_scenario = the concrete maintenance/rot/waste cost. Surface borderline candidates rather than dropping them.

<result>Consistent frontmatter format, good.

I've completed a thorough audit of this branch's diff against `main`.

## Audit Report — branch `chore/claude-housekeeping`

### 🔴 Hard violations
None found.

### 🟡 Concerns
None found. Everything checked out:

1. **CONTRIBUTING.md numbering (1–39 + 4a) is strictly increasing, no dupes/gaps.** Verified `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/CONTRIBUTING.md` lines 9–123: A(1–4+4a,5) → B(6–12) → C(13–17) → D(18–25) → E(26–29) → F(30–32, unchanged) → G(33–35, was 32–34) → H(36–39, was 35–38). No duplicate 32, no gaps.

2. **Cross-references to shifted rules all resolve correctly.**
   - `CLAUDE.md:340` "rule 39" → CONTRIBUTING.md rule 39 (quarterly MCP audit) — matches.
   - `.claude/skills/security-scan/SKILL.md:3,11` "rule 36" → end-of-week quick scan — matches (unshifted-content, correctly renumbered).
   - `.claude/skills/security-scan/SKILL.md:94` "rule 38" → "Security vulnerabilities are not public GitHub issues" — matches.
   - `docs/progress.md:364` "rule 32" → still correctly means the F-section read-modify-write rule (F section did **not** shift) — confirmed this citation was correctly left untouched.
   - `.claude/agents/migration-safety.md` was bumped from a stale `#24`/CLAUDE.md link to the correct `#30`/CONTRIBUTING.md link (rule 30 = backward-compatible migrations) — a bonus fix bundled into this housekeeping, not required by the G/H renumbering but correct.
   - `.claude/agents/docs-consistency-guard.md:54` still mentions `working-agreement #24` — but this is illustrative example text in the guard's own "Dead working-agreement references" checklist item ("e.g., working-agreement #3, working-agreement #24"), not a live citation asserting rule content — not a defect.

3. **Rule-count claims match reality.** `CONTRIBUTING.md` has exactly 39 numbered rules (+ 4a). `CLAUDE.md` (lines 132, 182, 276) and `docs/contributing.md` (line 3) all say "39 rules" — consistent. No remaining "37 rule(s)" references anywhere in the repo.

4. **All new relative links resolve.** Verified from `.claude/agents/`: `../../CLAUDE.md`, `../../CONTRIBUTING.md`, `../../docs/adr/README.md`, `../../docs/progress.md`, `../../context/post-v1-roadmap.md`, `../../.github/pull_request_template.md`, `../../.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `../../backend/tests/support/adversarial.py` — all exist. `docs/TODO.md` is referenced only as descriptive text ("was never adopted"), not as a link.

5. **CLAUDE.md section refs (§6, §9, §11, §13) still line up** with actual headers (checked via `grep "^## " CLAUDE.md`) — no numbering drift from this diff.

6. **New files (`qa-verifier.md`, `security-scan/SKILL.md`) are self-consistent** — frontmatter format matches existing agent/skill conventions, internal rule citations (4a, 3, 36, 38) all point at correct current rule content.

One cosmetic note (not a finding, no action needed): `docs-consistency-guard.md:58` says "fixed in #547" — issue #547 is currently **open** (this branch is the fix); the phrasing will become accurate once this PR merges and closes it.

### Cross-reference summary
- CONTRIBUTING.md numbered rules: 39 (1–39 + 4a) — matches all "39 rules"/"39-rule" claims repo-wide (3 in CLAUDE.md, 1 in docs/contributing.md).
- Rule-number citations checked repo-wide (`.claude/`, `.github/`, `docs/`, `CLAUDE.md`, `docs/adr/`, `frontend/mutation/README.md`, `context/post-v1-roadmap.md`): all resolve to correct rule content post-renumbering.
- Links checked in the diff's new/changed content: 8/8 resolve.

### ✅ Verdict
`Pass — documentation is consistent and references resolve.`

<result>Analysis complete. I read the full `main...HEAD` diff, the CI workflow (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`), `CONTRIBUTING.md` (rule numbering + rules 4a/13–17/36–39), and cross-checked rule-number citations across the agent/skill files. Here are the candidate findings.

---

## Candidate findings (code-review, instruction-file scope)

### 1. security-scan step 3 re-lists CI's `pip-audit` command but drops CI's `--ignore-vuln` list → weekly false positives + drift
- **File:** `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/.claude/skills/security-scan/SKILL.md:39`
- **Summary:** The skill's `pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt` claims to be "the same commands CI runs as a merge gate," but CI's `backend-audit` job (ci.yml) pins `pip-audit==2.10.0` and passes `--ignore-vuln PYSEC-2026-36/35 CVE-2026-26007/27448/27459` (5 advisories deliberately accepted, tracked in #129). The skill omits all of them.
- **failure_scenario:** Every weekly scan reports those 5 already-accepted advisories as fresh "🔴 findings," so the operator re-triages known noise each week and is trained to skim past pip-audit output — masking the real 6th vuln when it lands. And when CI's ignore-list changes (#129 removes them, or a new CVE is added), this hand-copied command silently diverges from the actual gate it claims to mirror. If a mirror is wanted, it should reference/inherit CI's flag set, not duplicate a subset of it.

### 2. security-scan hardcodes credential expiry dates + resource-group names in a security check → false "clean" on rotation
- **File:** `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/.claude/skills/security-scan/SKILL.md:71-82`
- **Summary:** Step 7's rotation register hardcodes `ADLS account SAS — expires 2027-06-28` and `Snowflake PAT — 90-day lifetime`, and steps 6's `az` commands hardcode `--resource-group dataq-rg` — while the step itself points at a durable source ("see the connections memory / Key Vault").
- **failure_scenario:** These are the fastest-rotting values in the whole PR. When the SAS is re-issued with a new expiry, the skill still asserts `2027-06-28`, the "expiring within 30 days" check silently never fires, and a *security* skill hands back false assurance on a live credential. If `dataq-rg` is renamed (the §13 log already shows churn toward shared/neutral naming), steps 6 both fail/skip. The durable fix is to read expiry from Key Vault, not restate it inline.

### 3. qa-verifier Mode 1 gate table duplicates ci.yml and already drifts (omits the pip-audit merge gate + CodeQL)
- **File:** `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/.claude/agents/qa-verifier.md:18-40`
- **Summary:** The Backend/Frontend gate tables re-list the commands that ci.yml defines and claim to "mirror CI's required checks," but the mirror is already incomplete: ci.yml has a `backend-audit` (`pip-audit`) merge gate and a CodeQL SAST gate that the table doesn't include (the audit lives only in the separate security-scan skill).
- **failure_scenario:** The whole point of the agent is "never use CI as the first feedback loop." A hand-maintained command mirror drifts the moment CI changes (new gate, changed env var, renamed script) — the author runs the local battery, gets all-green, and CI still fails on the un-mirrored gate, defeating the agent's reason to exist. A single-source approach (drive the same make/script targets CI invokes) would not rot.

### 4. CLAUDE.md §13 breadcrumb "rule 39 (numbered 38 pre-#547)" is a bandaid at the wrong altitude
- **File:** `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/CLAUDE.md:286` (the `#522` clause in the §13 narrative)
- **Summary:** A historical log entry now carries a live rule number *plus* a parenthetical re-mapping to the pre-renumber number. A §13 narrative log records what was true at the time; embedding a self-correcting rule-number citation inside it is patching one symptom of the renumber, not the class.
- **failure_scenario:** The next renumber turns this into "rule 40," leaving two stale numbers nested in one clause, and this breadcrumb only fixes this one citation while every other doc citing "rule 39" drifts unnoticed — giving false confidence that rule-number references are maintained. A dated log line should not cite a live rule number at all (or should link the rule by stable anchor, not ordinal).

### 5. gh-issue-from-finding hardcodes milestone titles → validation halt on rename, rot at Week 8
- **File:** `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/.claude/skills/gh-issue-from-finding/SKILL.md:21,92` (also example at :102)
- **Summary:** The skill correctly *derives* the current-week milestone from CLAUDE.md §13, but hardcodes the literal `Backlog (post-v1 / testing)` title (twice) and a `Week 7 — Deployment, hardening & docs` example. Step 2a then validates the resolved title against live milestones and *halts* if absent.
- **failure_scenario:** If the post-v1 backlog milestone is ever renamed, every post-v1 issue-filing halts at 2a validation with "milestone doesn't exist" — the skill hard-stops on a value it should be resolving, not asserting. The `Week 7 —` example rots the moment Week 8 opens (today is 2026-07-02, Week 7 in progress). Deriving the post-v1 milestone the same way it derives the week (query + match "post-v1"/"Backlog") removes both liabilities.

### 6. migration-safety "Why this matters" hardcodes deploy-pipeline specifics that will rot
- **File:** `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/.claude/agents/migration-safety.md:14`
- **Summary:** The rewritten rationale hardcodes `deployed to Azure production (since 2026-06-28)`, the `dataq-app-migrate` job name, and "api + worker Container Apps" ordering. This is more durable than the security-scan dates (it is ADR-0024-anchored and the mechanism is stable), so it is the most borderline of the six — surfaced per instruction to not drop borderline candidates.
- **failure_scenario:** The migrate job name / provider are exactly what the cloud-neutral cutover work (ADR 0028, already in-flight per §13, with AWS/GCP IaC slated post-v1) will change. When the migrate step is renamed or the provider generalizes, this paragraph asserts a job/provider that no longer exists, and the agent's stated "why" contradicts the actual pipeline. Pointing at the deploy workflow / ADR for the mechanism (keeping only the invariant: "migrate runs before old images finish rolling") would not rot.

---

Note on things that check out (not findings): the CONTRIBUTING renumber (#547) inserted a new rule 32 in section F, so rules 13–17 are unaffected — qa-verifier's "13–17 (CI gates)" citation (line 113) and migration-safety's "#30" are both correct against the new numbering, and security-scan's "rule 36"/"rule 38" citations are internally consistent. The rule-number citations are correct *today*; findings 4 and the broader pattern are about their fragility across the next renumber.</result>

<result>I've completed the cross-file trace. The renumbering is internally consistent almost everywhere — I verified every rule-number citation lands on the right content in the new numbering, all referenced paths/scripts/milestones/labels exist, the "39 rules" claim is correct, and the CI-mirror commands in security-scan match `.github/workflows/ci.yml` exactly. Here are the candidate findings, strongest first.

---

**Finding 1 — `#547` is still OPEN but two changed files describe the renumber as an already-landed fix "in #547"** (medium confidence)
- Files/lines: `.claude/agents/docs-consistency-guard.md:58` ("two rules were numbered 32, fixed in #547") and `CLAUDE.md:286` ("CONTRIBUTING rule 39 (numbered 38 pre-#547)").
- `gh issue view 547` → state OPEN, title "docs: CONTRIBUTING.md has two rules numbered 32 — G/H sections off by one…". #547 is the *bug report* of the duplicate; the actual renumber is this unmerged `chore/claude-housekeeping` branch, not a merged PR #547.
- failure_scenario: The docs-consistency-guard agent (or any reader) follows its own new rule "grep for rule references first", checks #547, finds it OPEN and titled "has two rules numbered 32", and concludes the duplicate was never fixed / the renumber was reverted — the opposite of reality. Also imprecise: "fixed in #547" attributes the fix to the issue number rather than the fixing PR. Fine once this PR merges and closes #547, but wrong on the branch as written.

**Finding 2 — docs-consistency-guard uses `working-agreement #24` as its illustrative "reference to validate" example** (low confidence)
- File/line: `.claude/agents/docs-consistency-guard.md:54` ('reference specific working-agreement numbers (e.g., "working-agreement #3", "working-agreement #24")').
- In the current numbering #24 = "Frontend linter: ESLint" — an unrelated rule, and notably #24 is the exact stale number this same PR just corrected in `migration-safety.md` (#24→#30). Using it as the guard's own example is confusing.
- failure_scenario: A maintainer reading the guard for how to spot dead references sees #24 held up as a canonical example and assumes #24 is a migration/security rule (its former neighborhood), mis-teaching the very check the file defines. Borderline — it is only an "e.g." placeholder, so arguably harmless.

**Finding 3 (cleared, surfaced per instructions) — `dataq-rg` hardcoded in `security-scan/SKILL.md`** (low confidence / likely NOT a violation)
- File/lines: `.claude/skills/security-scan/SKILL.md:79-82` (`az keyvault list --resource-group dataq-rg …`).
- CLAUDE.md §11 (line 256) forbids "no hardcoded Azure resource names/endpoints **in business logic**" and §256 scopes it to "route/service code". A read-only ops/security checklist skill is not business logic, so this does not match the quoted rule.
- failure_scenario: If the project later interprets §11 broadly (any git-tracked file), the `dataq-rg` literal would need parameterizing. As written the rule's "in business logic" qualifier exempts it. Flagging only because the task asked; I do not consider it a real violation.

**Finding 4 (cleared) — qa-verifier Mode 1 gate order differs from CONTRIBUTING rule 13's listed order** (very low confidence)
- File: `.claude/agents/qa-verifier.md` backend gate table (Format/black → Lint/ruff → …) vs `CONTRIBUTING.md:65` rule 13 ("Ruff → Black --check → mypy → Bandit → pytest").
- qa-verifier lists Black before Ruff; rule 13 lists Ruff first. qa-verifier explicitly says it orders by "fail-fast cheap ones first," and these are independent gates, so correctness is unaffected. Not a genuine contradiction; noted only for completeness.

---

**Everything else in scope checked out clean** (no findings):
- Every renumbered citation is correct: `security-scan` rule 36 (end-of-week scan ✓) and rule 38 (security-not-public ✓); `CLAUDE.md:340` + `:286` rule 39 (MCP audit ✓); `migration-safety.md` #30 links to CONTRIBUTING and #30 *is* "backward-compatible migrations only" ✓ (this is a fix — the old #24/CLAUDE.md link was wrong); `docs/progress.md:364` "rule 32" still correctly = read-modify-write ✓ (the renumber actually *disambiguated* it).
- "39 rules across 8 categories" is accurate: CONTRIBUTING.md has sequential rules 1–39, no gaps/dupes, categories A–H. `CLAUDE.md` (3 spots) and `docs/contributing.md` all updated to 39.
- Reference existence all pass: `backend/tests/support/adversarial.py`, `backend/scripts/e2e_smoke.py`, `backend/scripts/seed_dev.py`, `scripts/check-typecheck-deps.py`, `context/post-v1-roadmap.md`, `docs/progress.md`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, ADR 0024 all exist; frontend `package.json` has `format:check`, `lint`, `typecheck`, `test`, `e2e`; the `../../` links in `.claude/agents/*.md` resolve to repo root correctly.
- gh-issue-from-finding: default milestone string "Week 7 — Deployment, hardening & docs" and "Backlog (post-v1 / testing)" both exist as open milestones (exact match); all named labels (`bug, enhancement, documentation, refactor, test, ci, security, epic, dependencies, priority/P0–P3, week-N-carryover`) exist; #8 is CLOSED/NOT_PLANNED and #9 CLOSED/COMPLETED — both matching the skill's claims.
- No lingering contradiction on issue titles: `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug.md`=`fix:`, `feature.md`=`feat:`, and working-agreement #3 shows `--title "fix: <desc>"`, so the skill's new prefix rule is now *consistent* (it fixed a prior contradiction); no other doc still asserts plain-sentence titles.
- No stale `docs/TODO.md` references remain anywhere after the guard's removal of that workflow (other `*TODO.md` hits are the unrelated external `HARNESS_TODO.md`).
- security-scan's `pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt` and `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` match CI's gates verbatim.</result>

Most of the diff checks out: the CI command mirror in `qa-verifier.md` is exact, the `betterleaks` hook / `pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt` / `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` / `python -m backend.scripts.{seed_dev,e2e_smoke}` invocations all resolve, `az … --resource-group dataq-rg` matches the terraform default, the `rules/branches/main` API fix is correct (legacy `…/branches/main/protection` really does 404), the milestone titles and issue-#8-NOT_PLANNED claim are accurate, CONTRIBUTING is now cleanly numbered 1–39, and no lingering `docs/TODO.md` references survive the deletion.

Candidate findings below.

---

**Finding 1 — `#547` cited as a completed fix, but it is an open bug report**
- file: `.claude/agents/docs-consistency-guard.md` line 58 (and `CLAUDE.md` line 286: "CONTRIBUTING rule 39 (numbered 38 pre-#547)")
- summary: New Hard-violation #7 says the duplicate rule-32 was "fixed in #547", and §13 implies #547 performed the renumber — but `#547` is still **OPEN**, titled *"CONTRIBUTING.md has two rules numbered 32 — G/H sections off by one."* It's the bug report, not a merged fix (the fix is this very branch).
- failure_scenario: The docs-consistency-guard, invoked to check rule-number drift, treats #547 as a landed fix and won't recognize that the current branch is what closes it (so it won't flag the missing `Closes #547`); anyone following the breadcrumb to "see the fix in #547" lands on an unresolved issue. Misattributes an issue number as a fix PR.

**Finding 2 — milestone validation (open-only) contradicts the skill's own closed-milestone procedure**
- file: `.claude/skills/gh-issue-from-finding/SKILL.md` line 40 (Step 2a) vs line 92 (Rules)
- summary: Step 2a resolves `--milestone` against `gh api …/milestones` "(open milestones only)" and, if not found, "stop and tell the user … do not file." But the Rules section (line 92) documents a procedure for assigning an issue to an **already-closed** milestone via the REST API.
- failure_scenario: A user asks to file a finding against a closed milestone (e.g. backfilling `Week 6 — Results dashboard + alerting`). Step 2a validates against open milestones only, doesn't find it, and hard-stops without filing — even though the same skill's Rules say closed-milestone assignment is supported. The two sections give opposite instructions; the agent that reads top-to-bottom refuses a request the skill claims to handle.

**Finding 3 — default milestone lifted from §13 "Current week:" won't string-match the milestone title**
- file: `.claude/skills/gh-issue-from-finding/SKILL.md` line 21 (Usage) / line 40 (Step 2a)
- summary: Default milestone = "the current feature-week milestone from CLAUDE.md §13". But §13's literal `Current week:` text is `Week 7 of 8 — Deployment, hardening & docs — IN PROGRESS…`, whereas the actual milestone title is `Week 7 — Deployment, hardening & docs` (no "of 8", no status). Step 2a does an exact title match and stops on mismatch.
- failure_scenario: An agent that derives the default by reading §13 (rather than using the parenthetical example) extracts `Week 7 of 8 — …`, fails the exact-title check in Step 2a, and — because the skill now mandates "do not file milestone-less" — files **no issue at all**, silently dropping the finding the skill exists to capture. New brittleness introduced by making milestone a hard, exact-match requirement.

**Finding 4 (borderline) — stale example `working-agreement #24` now resolves to ESLint after this PR's renumber**
- file: `.claude/agents/docs-consistency-guard.md` line 54 (Hard-violation #5; unchanged line, but its meaning is broken by this PR's CONTRIBUTING renumber)
- summary: The "Dead working-agreement references" check uses `"working-agreement #24"` as its illustrative example of a migration-ish reference. This PR renumbered rules so `#24` is now **"Frontend linter: ESLint"** (the migration rule moved to #30 — which is exactly why `migration-safety.md` was updated #24→#30 in this same diff). The example in the guard was not updated in lockstep.
- failure_scenario: The example is now misleading — an agent using #24 as the template for "a working-agreement reference to verify" checks the wrong rule; and because #24 still exists (as ESLint), the example no longer illustrates a *dead* reference at all, weakening the check it's meant to teach.</result>

<result>I now have all the evidence needed. Here are my verdicts.

**C1 — CONFIRMED.** The skill's step 3 runs bare `pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt` and claims "These are the same commands CI runs as a merge gate," but ci.yml's backend-audit job runs pip-audit with five `--ignore-vuln` flags (`PYSEC-2026-36`, `PYSEC-2026-35`, `CVE-2026-26007`, `CVE-2026-27448`, `CVE-2026-27459`) for advisories accepted under #129 — the skill omits all five, so the weekly scan re-reports those accepted advisories as findings.

**C2 — CONFIRMED.** Mode 1 states "The gates below mirror CI's required checks (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`)," but the gate tables list only lint/format/typecheck/SAST/tests for each side and omit ci.yml's `backend-audit` (pip-audit), `frontend-audit` (pnpm audit), and `codeql` jobs — so an author with a green local battery can still hit a CI failure on any of those un-mirrored jobs (e.g. a newly published CVE or a `py/side-effect-in-assert` CodeQL alert).

**C3 — CONFIRMED.** Genuine top-to-bottom contradiction: Step 2a validates the milestone against `gh api .../milestones` "(open milestones only)" and says "If it doesn't exist, stop... do not file milestone-less," while the Rules section documents assigning an already-closed milestone via `gh api -X PATCH ... -F milestone=<num>`; closed milestones exist (e.g. "Week 6 — Results dashboard + alerting | closed"), so an agent following Step 2a refuses the closed-milestone backfill the Rules claim to support.

**C4 — PLAUSIBLE.** §13's literal headline is "Week 7 of 8 — Deployment, hardening & docs — IN PROGRESS…" while the real open milestone title is exactly "Week 7 — Deployment, hardening & docs" (`gh api` confirmed), so a literal transcription of §13 would fail Step 2a's exact-match; however the skill's own Usage line hands over the correct string inline — "the current feature-week milestone from CLAUDE.md §13 (e.g. `Week 7 — Deployment, hardening & docs`)" — so the "no issue filed" outcome only occurs if the agent trusts §13's decorated headline over the skill's parenthetical, making it a realistic-but-mitigated path.

**C5 — REFUTED.** Rule 24 is `Frontend linter: ESLint` (CONTRIBUTING.md:83) both before and after this PR — the renumber only touched rules 33–39, never #24 — and item 5 lists `#24` merely as an example of a reference number that must resolve to a real rule, which it does; nothing in the unchanged example asserts #24 is "dead," so the check is not mis-taught (migration-safety was corrected from #24 to #30 because #24 was always ESLint, not the migrations rule).

**C6 — REFUTED.** The branch's second commit body reads "Fixes #547," so on merge #547 closes as the resolved duplicate-32 bug report; in the intended post-merge steady state a reader following #547 finds a closed/resolved issue and correctly concludes the duplicate is fixed, so the "open bug ⇒ concludes unfixed" concern exists only transiently during review.</result>

input.old_string:
### 3. Local dependency audit (mirror of the synchronous CI gate)

```bash
pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt
cd frontend && pnpm audit --audit-level=high
```

These are the same commands CI runs as a merge gate — running them here catches vulns published since the last PR merged.
input.new_string:
### 3. Local dependency audit (mirror of the synchronous CI gate)

Mirror CI **exactly**: read the current invocations from `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (`backend-audit` and `frontend-audit` jobs) and run them verbatim — including the pinned `pip-audit` version and the `--ignore-vuln` flags for already-accepted advisories (tracked in #129). A bare `pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt` re-reports the accepted advisories as noise, training the reader to skim past real ones. Frontend: `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` from `frontend/`.

Running these here catches vulns published since the last PR merged. An advisory on CI's ignore list is accepted risk — mention it under "known/accepted", never as a new finding.

input.command:
tmp=$(mktemp); cat > "$tmp" <<'EOF'
## Summary

- Refreshes the project's `.claude` agents/skills to match current project state: migration-safety cited working-agreement #24 (= ESLint) instead of #30, docs-consistency-guard audited branch protection via the legacy API (404s — protection is a ruleset) and maintained a never-adopted `docs/TODO.md`, gh-issue-from-finding contradicted the settled issue-title convention and never assigned milestones.
- Adds two new assets: the **qa-verifier** agent (local CI-mirror gate battery + test-quality audit + application data-testing mode with scratch-data cleanup contract) and the **/security-scan** skill (end-of-week scan per working-agreement 36).
- Fixes the CONTRIBUTING.md duplicate rule #32: G/H renumbered to 33–39, cross-references updated (CLAUDE.md "rule 38"→39, "37 rules"→39 ×3, docs/contributing.md).

## Linked issue

Fixes #547

## Type of change

- [ ] feat — new functionality
- [ ] fix — bug fix (linked issue above)
- [x] chore — repo housekeeping / tooling
- [x] docs — documentation only
- [ ] refactor — no behaviour change
- [ ] test — test-only change

## Checklist

- [x] **Manually tested locally** (verification greps below; no runtime surface — markdown only)
- [x] **Single functionality** — one housekeeping concern: .claude assets + the doc-numbering defect they exposed
- [x] **Conventional commit title** (`feat:`, `fix:`, `chore:`, `docs:`, `test:`, `refactor:`)
- [x] **No secrets, credentials, or `.env` files committed**
